clinicalStem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with a 6-hour history of high fever (39.8°C), drooling, and progressive difficulty swallowing. He is sitting bolt upright and leaning forward with his neck extended ('sniffing position'). His voice is muffled and he refuses to open his mouth. He appears anxious and toxic. Respiratory rate is 32/min with soft inspiratory stridor. His immunisation status is unclear. Which of the following is the MOST APPROPRIATE immediate next step?
options: Perform direct laryngoscopy at the bedside to inspect the epiglottis
Arrange lateral neck X-ray to confirm the 'thumbprint sign' before any intervention
Secure the airway in the operating theatre under controlled conditions with anaesthesia and ENT standby
Administer nebulised adrenaline and observe for 2 hours
Obtain blood cultures and start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate immediately
explanation: The clinical picture — toxic child, drooling, muffled voice, sniffing posture, stridor — is classic acute epiglottitis, most likely Haemophilus influenzae type b in an unimmunised child. The immediate priority is securing the airway safely; any attempt at bedside inspection or stimulation can precipitate complete obstruction and cardiac arrest. The child must be kept calm and taken directly to the operating theatre for controlled intubation with anaesthetic and ENT/surgical tracheotomy backup available. Lateral neck X-ray and blood cultures are deferred until the airway is secured.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Bedside laryngoscopy in a child with suspected epiglottitis is dangerous — it can trigger complete airway obstruction and respiratory arrest and must never be performed outside a controlled operative setting.

Although the thumbprint sign on lateral neck X-ray is characteristic, obtaining imaging delays definitive airway management and the stress of the procedure can precipitate acute obstruction; it is not the next step.
Correct: the safest and most appropriate immediate step is controlled airway management in the operating theatre with full anaesthetic and surgical backup.
Nebulised adrenaline is used for croup (viral laryngotracheobronchitis), not epiglottitis; it provides no benefit for supraglottic bacterial infection and wastes critical time.
Oral antibiotics are insufficient for severe epiglottitis and cannot be given safely to a child who is drooling and unable to swallow; IV antibiotics follow — not precede — airway control.
clinicalPearl: In suspected acute epiglottitis, do NOT examine the throat or agitate the child — keep calm and go directly to the operating theatre for controlled intubation.
keyClue: Toxic child, drooling, sniffing posture, muffled voice, inspiratory stridor, unclear immunisation status
commonExamTrap: Ordering a lateral neck X-ray before securing the airway — imaging is secondary to airway control in an unstable child with epiglottitis.
missedConcept: Epiglottitis is a true airway emergency; the management priority is a controlled, definitive airway — not investigation.
redFlagReason: Complete upper airway obstruction and respiratory arrest can occur within minutes if the child is disturbed or the airway is not secured urgently.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old male heavy smoker (40 pack-years) presents with a 4-month history of persistent hoarseness. He denies dysphagia, odynophagia, or haemoptysis. He drinks two glasses of whisky daily. On indirect laryngoscopy the vocal cords appear thickened and erythematous with irregular whitish plaques on the left cord. Cervical lymph nodes are not palpable. Which single investigation is MOST important at this point?
options: CT scan of the neck and chest
Microlaryngoscopy with biopsy of the left vocal cord lesion
Trial of proton pump inhibitor therapy for 8 weeks and review
Flexible nasendoscopy in the outpatient clinic
explanation: A smoker over 40 years old with hoarseness persisting beyond 3 weeks and a visible leukoplakic lesion on the vocal cord must be assumed to have malignancy until proven otherwise. Microlaryngoscopy under general anaesthesia with biopsy provides histological diagnosis, which is the essential step to distinguish between benign chronic laryngitis, dysplasia, and squamous cell carcinoma. No imaging or empirical treatment should delay tissue diagnosis in this setting.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT of the neck and chest is valuable for staging once malignancy is confirmed, but tissue diagnosis must come first; CT cannot replace histology for diagnosis.
Correct: microlaryngoscopy with biopsy is the gold standard for evaluating suspicious vocal cord lesions in a high-risk patient — it yields histological diagnosis.
Sputum cytology has low sensitivity for glottic lesions and cannot provide a definitive histological diagnosis; it is not adequate for this clinical scenario.
A PPI trial may be appropriate for mild dysphonia attributable to laryngopharyngeal reflux, but leukoplakia in a heavy smoker requires urgent tissue diagnosis, not empirical treatment.
Flexible nasendoscopy can visualise the lesion but does not allow biopsy; in an outpatient setting it defers — rather than provides — the definitive investigation.
clinicalPearl: Any hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks in a smoker with a visible vocal cord lesion demands urgent microlaryngoscopy and biopsy to exclude squamous cell carcinoma.
keyClue: 40 pack-year smoker, 4-month hoarseness, leukoplakic plaque on vocal cord
commonExamTrap: Starting a PPI trial without histological assessment — empirical reflux treatment is appropriate only when malignancy has been excluded.
missedConcept: Leukoplakia of the vocal cord in a smoker is a pre-malignant lesion that mandates biopsy, not watchful waiting.

clinicalStem: A 2-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department at 2 AM with a sudden-onset barking cough and harsh inspiratory stridor that woke her from sleep. She is afebrile (temperature 37.4°C). Her oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. She is alert, looks towards her mother, and is mildly distressed but not toxic. There is mild sub-costal recession. She had a mild coryzal illness for 2 days prior. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: This is classic mild-to-moderate viral croup: a toddler aged 6 months to 3 years, nocturnal onset following an upper respiratory tract infection, characteristic barking (seal-like) cough, and stridor with mild recession. The child is alert, non-toxic, afebrile, and maintaining saturations — all features of croup rather than the more dangerous bacterial conditions. Parainfluenza virus is the most common cause.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Epiglottitis presents with a toxic, high-fever child who drools, sits upright, has a muffled voice, and refuses to lie down — none of these features are present here.
Foreign body aspiration typically causes sudden choking during play or eating, may produce asymmetric wheeze or reduced air entry, and lacks the preceding URTI and barking cough.
Bacterial tracheitis (Staphylococcal) produces high fever, toxic appearance, copious purulent secretions, and rapidly progressing respiratory distress — far more severe than described.
Correct: classic mild-moderate croup — toddler, nocturnal barking cough, stridor after URTI, alert and non-toxic with mild recession only.
Retropharyngeal abscess causes muffled voice, drooling, odynophagia, neck stiffness, and fever; stridor is uncommon and a barking cough is not a feature.
clinicalPearl: Croup = toddler + post-URTI + nocturnal barking cough + inspiratory stridor; treat mild-moderate cases with oral dexamethasone.
keyClue: 2-year-old, nocturnal barking cough and stridor following 2-day coryzal illness, non-toxic, afebrile
commonExamTrap: Confusing croup with epiglottitis — croup is viral, afebrile/low-grade fever, barking cough; epiglottitis is bacterial, high fever, drooling, muffled voice, no barking cough.
missedConcept: The barking cough and preceding URTI are the cardinal distinguishing features of viral croup.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old male retired factory worker with a 35 pack-year smoking history presents to his GP with a 6-week history of hoarseness. He has no dysphagia, no neck swelling, and no weight loss. He drinks alcohol socially. Examination of the neck reveals no palpable lymphadenopathy. Flexible nasendoscopy shows a small irregular lesion confined to the right true vocal cord with normal cord mobility. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: A male smoker over 60 presenting with unexplained hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks has laryngeal carcinoma until proven otherwise. An irregular lesion on the true vocal cord without nodal involvement is most consistent with early (T1) glottic squamous cell carcinoma. The absence of lymphadenopathy fits — glottic tumours have a poor lymphatic supply and nodal spread is typically late. Microlaryngoscopy with biopsy is needed to confirm.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Vocal cord polyps appear as smooth, pale, translucent swellings — usually unilateral and related to vocal overuse; they do not have an irregular surface and are less likely in a heavy smoker with a 6-week history.
Reinke's oedema presents as bilateral, diffuse gelatinous swelling of both cords giving a low-pitched voice, typically in female smokers; it is not an irregular lesion confined to one cord.
Correct: an irregular unilateral vocal cord lesion with persistent hoarseness in a heavy smoker is glottic SCC until proven otherwise by biopsy.
Vocal process granulomas arise at the posterior vocal process from intubation trauma or LPR; they have a smooth, mound-like appearance and are not irregular infiltrating lesions.

Recurrent respiratory papillomatosis (HPV-related) appears as multiple wart-like papillary lesions typically on both cords, usually in younger patients; it is less likely in a 62-year-old heavy smoker with an irregular lesion.
clinicalPearl: Hoarseness persisting more than 3 weeks in a smoker demands urgent laryngoscopy — glottic SCC presents early with hoarseness because the vocal cord vibration is disrupted even by small tumours.
keyClue: 62-year-old male, 35 pack-year smoker, 6-week hoarseness, irregular unilateral vocal cord lesion, no lymphadenopathy
commonExamTrap: Assuming the absence of lymphadenopathy argues against cancer — glottic tumours characteristically lack early nodal spread due to the sparse lymphatics of the true cord.
missedConcept: The true vocal cord has a poor lymphatic supply, so early glottic SCC presents with hoarseness alone and without nodal disease — making early diagnosis possible.

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old male with a 40-year smoking history presents with a 3-month history of progressive dysphagia to solids, a 'hot potato' voice (muffled, as if speaking with food in the mouth), and referred otalgia to the right ear. On examination a 2 cm hard lymph node is palpable at level II of the right neck. Flexible nasendoscopy reveals a bulky, ulcerated lesion at the epiglottis and aryepiglottic fold. What is the SINGLE MOST APPROPRIATE next investigation?
options: Ultrasound-guided fine-needle aspiration cytology of the neck node
CT scan of the neck, chest, and abdomen with contrast
Panendoscopy under general anaesthesia with biopsy of the primary lesion
explanation: The clinical presentation is strongly suggestive of supraglottic squamous cell carcinoma (muffled voice, dysphagia, referred otalgia, visible epiglottic-aryepiglottic lesion with ipsilateral nodal disease). The most important next step is tissue diagnosis via panendoscopy (laryngoscopy, pharyngoscopy, oesophagoscopy) under general anaesthesia with biopsy of the primary — this confirms the histological diagnosis, assesses the extent of mucosal disease, and identifies synchronous primaries. Staging CT follows once the diagnosis is confirmed.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Barium swallow can demonstrate pharyngeal or oesophageal lesions but cannot obtain histology; it is a functional imaging study, not a diagnostic one for suspected malignancy.
FNAC of the neck node may confirm nodal metastasis but does not provide the primary site diagnosis needed for definitive staging and treatment planning; it is a secondary investigation.
Contrast CT of neck, chest, and abdomen is essential for staging after tissue diagnosis, but imaging alone does not provide histology and cannot be the first investigation.

Correct: panendoscopy with biopsy gives histological diagnosis of the primary, maps mucosal extent, and identifies synchronous primaries in the upper aerodigestive tract — it is the cornerstone investigation.
PET-CT is a staging and surveillance tool used after histological diagnosis is established; it is not the immediate next investigation when the primary lesion is visible and accessible.
clinicalPearl: Supraglottic SCC: muffled ('hot potato') voice + referred otalgia + dysphagia + early cervical lymphadenopathy (Level II/III) — tissue diagnosis by panendoscopy under GA is the cornerstone.
keyClue: Hot potato voice, referred otalgia, dysphagia, visible epiglottic ulcerated lesion, hard ipsilateral level II node
commonExamTrap: Jumping to staging CT before tissue diagnosis — histopathology from biopsy must come first to confirm malignancy and tumour type.
missedConcept: Supraglottic tumours drain richly to cervical lymph nodes and often present with nodal metastasis even when the primary is small; referred otalgia via Arnold's nerve (CN X) is a classic symptom.

clinicalStem: A 70-year-old male presents with a 5-month history of progressive exertional breathlessness and a gradually worsening biphasic stridor. He also reports a recent change in voice quality but denies dysphagia. He has smoked 30 cigarettes per day for 45 years. CXR shows no parenchymal lesion. Flexible nasendoscopy initially appears normal from above, but careful inspection below the cords reveals a circumferential reddish mass in the subglottic space causing significant luminal narrowing. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Extrinsic tracheal compression from thyroid goitre
explanation: An elderly heavy smoker with progressive biphasic stridor (indicating fixed upper airway obstruction), dysphonia, and a visible circumferential subglottic mass has subglottic squamous cell carcinoma until proven otherwise. Subglottic SCC is the rarest of the three laryngeal subsites and characteristically presents late with stridor (rather than early hoarseness) because the lesion does not affect cord vibration until it extends upwards. CT and biopsy under microlaryngoscopy are needed for confirmation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Post-intubation tracheal stenosis presents as a smooth, circumferential narrowing and requires a history of prolonged intubation or tracheostomy — not present here.
Correct: a circumferential subglottic mass in an elderly heavy smoker causing progressive biphasic stridor is subglottic SCC until proven otherwise.
Extrinsic tracheal compression from goitre causes smooth, extrinsic narrowing on imaging with a palpable neck mass and deviation of the trachea; the mass here is intrinsic and intraluminal.
GPA (Wegener's) can cause subglottic stenosis but typically occurs in younger patients with systemic features (renal, pulmonary) and ANCA positivity; the lesion is inflammatory/stenotic, not a mass.
Subglottic haemangiomas are lesions of infants and young children, not elderly smokers; they are not seen in this age group.
clinicalPearl: Subglottic SCC presents late with biphasic stridor rather than early hoarseness — think of it in any elderly smoker with progressive unexplained stridor and a subglottic mass.
keyClue: Elderly heavy smoker, progressive biphasic stridor, circumferential subglottic mass on nasendoscopy
commonExamTrap: Assuming hoarseness is the presenting symptom of all laryngeal cancers — subglottic SCC often spares voice until late and presents with stridor from obstruction.
missedConcept: The three laryngeal subsites have different lymphatic drainage and different natural histories: subglottic tumours are rarest, present late, and have bilateral lymphatic drainage — worse prognosis.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old Chinese man presents with a 3-month history of unilateral left-sided nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and a painless 3 cm hard mass in the left posterior triangle of the neck. He also reports intermittent left-sided hearing loss and a sense of fullness in the left ear. He is a non-smoker. On examination there is a firm, non-tender cervical node at level V. Flexible nasendoscopy reveals a mucosal irregularity in the left fossa of Rosenmüller. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: This presentation is classic for nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC): a young-to-middle-aged patient of South-East Asian or Chinese ethnicity presenting with the triad of unilateral nasal obstruction/epistaxis, unilateral secretory otitis media (Eustachian tube obstruction), and cervical lymphadenopathy (especially posterior triangle, level V). EBV infection is a key aetiological factor. The fossa of Rosenmüller is the most common primary site. NPC must be suspected and biopsied urgently.
wrongAnswerExplanations: NHL can present with cervical lymphadenopathy and nasal symptoms but would not cause unilateral middle ear effusion via Eustachian tube obstruction, and the nasopharyngeal lesion in the fossa of Rosenmüller points strongly to an epithelial primary.
Branchial cysts are smooth, fluctuant, benign lateral neck swellings presenting in young adults; they are not hard, not associated with nasal obstruction or ear symptoms, and not posterior triangle.

Correct: South-East Asian male + unilateral nasal obstruction + epistaxis + unilateral middle ear effusion + posterior triangle (level V) hard node + nasopharyngeal lesion = NPC until proven otherwise.
Adenoid hypertrophy causes bilateral nasal obstruction in children and may cause bilateral Eustachian tube dysfunction; it does not cause a hard posterior triangle node or epistaxis in an adult.

Infectious mononucleosis causes generalised, soft, tender bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy with pharyngitis, tonsillar exudate, and splenomegaly; it does not produce a unilateral posterior triangle hard mass or nasal/ear symptoms.
clinicalPearl: NPC triad: unilateral epistaxis/nasal obstruction + unilateral glue ear (ETT obstruction) + posterior triangle (level V) cervical node — especially in an Asian patient. Fossa of Rosenmüller is the classic site.
keyClue: Chinese male, unilateral epistaxis, nasal obstruction, unilateral middle ear effusion, hard posterior triangle node, nasopharyngeal lesion
commonExamTrap: Attributing the middle ear effusion to a separate cause — in NPC it results from tumour occluding the ipsilateral Eustachian tube orifice.
missedConcept: NPC is EBV-associated and endemic in South-East Asia; it metastasises early to posterior cervical (level V) lymph nodes even with a small primary that may not be easily visible.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old woman with a history of long-standing iron deficiency anaemia and dysphagia presents with progressive dysphagia that has now worsened to include liquids and is associated with a 8 kg weight loss over 3 months. She was previously known to have Plummer-Vinson (Patterson-Kelly-Brown) syndrome. Examination reveals a 2 cm right-level II cervical node. Flexible nasendoscopy shows a posterior pharyngeal wall lesion at the level of the piriform sinus. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE next investigation?
options: CT of the neck, chest, and abdomen
Panendoscopy under general anaesthesia with biopsy
explanation: Plummer-Vinson syndrome is a pre-malignant condition strongly associated with hypopharyngeal and oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma. This patient now has progressive dysphagia, significant weight loss, cervical lymphadenopathy, and a visible piriform sinus lesion — strongly suggesting hypopharyngeal SCC. Panendoscopy under GA with biopsy of the primary lesion is the essential investigation: it provides histological diagnosis, maps the mucosal extent of the tumour, and allows detection of synchronous primary tumours in the upper aerodigestive tract, which are found in up to 10% of cases.
wrongAnswerExplanations: OGD alone evaluates the oesophagus but does not provide full assessment of the hypopharynx under direct vision, does not allow adequate biopsy of the piriform sinus, and misses simultaneous pharyngeal lesions.
CT of neck, chest, and abdomen is the staging investigation of choice once histological diagnosis is confirmed, but it cannot provide tissue diagnosis and should not be the first step.

Barium swallow provides functional and anatomical information about the swallowing mechanism but cannot obtain tissue diagnosis; it is inferior to panendoscopy for evaluating a suspected malignant lesion.
Correct: panendoscopy with biopsy provides histological diagnosis and maps mucosal extent; it identifies synchronous primaries and is the definitive diagnostic step before staging.

Iron studies and ferritin would confirm the iron deficiency of Plummer-Vinson syndrome but are irrelevant to investigating the suspected malignancy — the haematological abnormality is already established.
clinicalPearl: Plummer-Vinson (Patterson-Kelly-Brown) syndrome = iron deficiency anaemia + dysphagia + oesophageal/hypopharyngeal web — it is pre-malignant and carries a significant risk of hypopharyngeal SCC.
keyClue: Plummer-Vinson syndrome, progressive dysphagia, weight loss, piriform sinus lesion, cervical lymphadenopathy
commonExamTrap: Treating the iron deficiency and assuming that explains the dysphagia — in Plummer-Vinson syndrome, new or progressive dysphagia mandates urgent malignancy investigation.
missedConcept: Hypopharyngeal SCC has the worst prognosis of all head and neck cancers; early biopsy and staging are critical; synchronous primaries must always be excluded by panendoscopy.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old non-smoking male presents with a 2-month history of right-sided sore throat and right otalgia (referred ear pain). He is a non-drinker but has a history of multiple sexual partners. On examination there is a 3 cm hard, non-tender right level II lymph node. Inspection of the oropharynx reveals a subtle asymmetry of the right tonsil with mild surface irregularity, but no obvious ulceration. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Tonsillar hyperplasia secondary to chronic tonsillitis
Branchial cleft cyst presenting with secondary infection
explanation: HPV-related oropharyngeal SCC (typically HPV-16) has a distinct epidemiological profile: younger, non-smoking, non-drinking males with a history of oral sex. It commonly presents with a cystic or hard cervical lymph node as the first sign, with a subtle or occult primary in the tonsillar tissue or base of tongue. The combination of young non-smoking male, sore throat with referred otalgia, hard ipsilateral level II node, and tonsillar asymmetry is the hallmark presentation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Peritonsillar abscess causes severe odynophagia, muffled (hot potato) voice, drooling, trismus, uvular deviation, and fever — this is an acute presentation very different from 2 months of mild symptoms.

Tonsillar lymphoma can present with tonsillar swelling and cervical nodes, but is typically bilateral, the nodes are rubbery rather than hard, and systemic B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) are usually present.
Correct: young non-smoking male + sexual history + right otalgia + hard level II node + tonsillar asymmetry = HPV-associated oropharyngeal SCC until proven otherwise.
Tonsillar hyperplasia in chronic tonsillitis is usually bilateral, causes recurrent acute sore throats with fevers, and does not produce a hard level II neck node.
Branchial cyst at level II is fluctuant and smooth-walled; even when infected it does not cause tonsillar asymmetry or referred otalgia via Arnold's nerve.
clinicalPearl: HPV-related oropharyngeal SCC: young non-smoking male + sexual risk factors + hard level II neck node + tonsillar asymmetry — cervical node is often the presenting finding with an occult primary.
keyClue: Non-smoking male, multiple sexual partners, 2-month sore throat, referred otalgia, hard level II node, tonsillar asymmetry
commonExamTrap: Assuming all head and neck cancers are in elderly smokers — HPV-16 oropharyngeal SCC is a distinct entity in younger non-smokers with sexual risk factors.
missedConcept: HPV-related oropharyngeal SCC (p16-positive) has a better prognosis than HPV-negative tumours and is increasing in incidence in Western countries among younger patients.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman wakes up one morning with sudden loss of hearing in her right ear associated with tinnitus and mild vertigo. She has no preceding URTI, no otalgia, and no history of trauma. She is otherwise healthy and on no medications. On examination the right ear canal and tympanic membrane are normal. Rinne's test is positive (AC>BC) bilaterally, and Weber lateralises to the LEFT ear. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
options: Right-sided acute otitis media with effusion
Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss secondary to medication
Cerumen impaction in the right ear
explanation: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss is defined as a sensorineural loss of at least 30 dB over 3 or more consecutive frequencies occurring within 72 hours. The clinical features — sudden unilateral hearing loss on waking, tinnitus, mild vertigo, normal otoscopy, Rinne positive bilaterally (AC>BC in both ears, confirming no conductive pathology), and Weber lateralising to the BETTER (left) ear — confirm a right-sided sensorineural loss. SSNHL is an otological emergency requiring urgent MRI to exclude acoustic neuroma and high-dose systemic corticosteroids.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute otitis media with effusion causes conductive hearing loss; Rinne's test would be negative on the right (BC>AC) and Weber would lateralise to the RIGHT (affected) side — the opposite of what is found here.
Correct: sudden right-sided sensorineural hearing loss — Rinne positive bilaterally, Weber to the LEFT (better) ear, normal otoscopy, sudden onset on waking.
Left-sided conductive loss would cause Weber to lateralise to the LEFT and Rinne to be negative on the left; this does not match the findings.
Bilateral ototoxic hearing loss from medication (e.g., aminoglycosides) affects both ears, is usually gradual, and requires a medication history — not described here.
Cerumen impaction causes a mild conductive hearing loss; otoscopy would reveal the impaction, and it would not cause tinnitus or vertigo.
clinicalPearl: Weber lateralises to the BETTER ear in sensorineural loss and to the WORSE ear in conductive loss — SSNHL is an emergency: begin steroids within 72 hours and arrange urgent MRI IAM.
keyClue: Sudden unilateral hearing loss on waking, tinnitus, vertigo, normal otoscopy, Rinne positive bilaterally, Weber to the opposite (left/better) ear
commonExamTrap: Forgetting Weber lateralisation rules — in unilateral SNHL, Weber goes to the BETTER (opposite) ear, not the affected ear.
missedConcept: SSNHL is an otological emergency — delay in starting corticosteroids worsens the prognosis; MRI of the internal auditory meati excludes acoustic neuroma.
redFlagReason: SSNHL requires urgent assessment and treatment within 72 hours; delay in corticosteroid therapy significantly reduces hearing recovery, and MRI is needed to exclude acoustic neuroma as a treatable cause.

clinicalStem: A 32-year-old woman presents with progressive bilateral hearing loss that has worsened since her last pregnancy 18 months ago. She denies tinnitus and vertigo. Her family history is positive for hearing loss in her mother and maternal aunt. On examination both tympanic membranes are intact and appear normal. Rinne's test is negative bilaterally (BC>AC). Weber lateralises to the right. Pure tone audiometry shows a bilateral conductive hearing loss with a characteristic dip at 2000 Hz (Carhart's notch). What investigation best confirms the diagnosis?
options: MRI of the internal auditory meati
CT of the temporal bones without contrast
explanation: The clinical picture — young woman, progressive bilateral conductive hearing loss, family history, worsening with pregnancy, Carhart's notch on audiometry, and intact tympanic membranes — is classic otosclerosis. Tympanometry is the best confirmatory investigation at this stage: it typically shows a reduced or flat (As or 'stiff') tympanogram with low compliance and absent ipsilateral acoustic reflexes, consistent with fixation of the stapes footplate. This non-invasive test confirms stapes fixation before surgical intervention (stapedectomy) is considered.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI of the IAM is used to exclude retrocochlear pathology (acoustic neuroma) in sensorineural hearing loss; otosclerosis is a conductive disorder and MRI is not the primary diagnostic investigation.

CT of temporal bones can show foci of lucency around the oval window in otosclerosis and is useful pre-operatively, but tympanometry is the simpler, non-invasive first-line confirmatory test in the diagnostic workup.
OAEs test outer hair cell function and are absent in sensorineural hearing loss; they may be present or absent in conductive loss but are not the best test for diagnosing otosclerosis specifically.
Correct: tympanometry in otosclerosis shows a characteristic flat/stiff (As) trace with very low compliance and absent stapedial reflexes — confirming stapes fixation.

ABR assesses the auditory pathway from cochlea to brainstem; it is used for neurodiagnosis (acoustic neuroma, auditory neuropathy) and threshold estimation in children — not for diagnosing otosclerosis.
clinicalPearl: Otosclerosis: young woman + bilateral conductive loss + family history + worsened by pregnancy + intact TM + Carhart's notch at 2000 Hz + As tympanogram with absent stapedial reflexes.
keyClue: Young woman, bilateral conductive hearing loss, family history, Carhart's notch at 2000 Hz, worsened with pregnancy
commonExamTrap: Ordering an MRI for conductive hearing loss — MRI evaluates sensorineural/retrocochlear pathology; for stapes fixation, tympanometry and CT temporal bone are the appropriate investigations.
missedConcept: Carhart's notch (maximum conductive dip at 2000 Hz) on PTA is pathognomonic of otosclerosis; absent stapedial reflexes on tympanometry confirm stapes fixation.

clinicalStem: A 50-year-old man presents with a 12-month history of gradually progressive unilateral right-sided sensorineural hearing loss associated with tinnitus and intermittent imbalance. He denies episodic vertigo. Rinne's test is positive on both sides; Weber lateralises to the left. Examination of the ear is entirely normal. Pure tone audiometry confirms a right-sided high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss. Which single investigation will MOST DEFINITIVELY establish the diagnosis?
options: CT scan of the temporal bones with contrast
MRI of the internal auditory meati (IAM) with gadolinium
explanation: The clinical picture — unilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss, unilateral tinnitus, and mild imbalance in a middle-aged patient — is the classic presentation of a vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma). MRI of the internal auditory meati with gadolinium contrast is the definitive gold-standard investigation. It can detect lesions as small as 2–3 mm within the IAM and accurately characterises the tumour size, location, and relationship to surrounding structures. CT and ABR can suggest the diagnosis but cannot definitively confirm it.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CT temporal bone with contrast can demonstrate bony expansion of the IAM but has much lower sensitivity for intracanalicular tumours less than 1 cm compared to gadolinium-enhanced MRI; it is not the gold standard.

ABR is a useful screening tool for acoustic neuroma — an abnormal interwave latency (particularly I–V) suggests retrocochlear pathology — but it has lower sensitivity than MRI and cannot definitively characterise the lesion.
Correct: MRI of the IAM with gadolinium is the gold standard for diagnosing acoustic neuroma; it detects even small intracanalicular lesions with high sensitivity and specificity.

ENG (electronystagmography) tests semicircular canal function via caloric testing and can show canal paresis on the affected side in acoustic neuroma, but it does not visualise the tumour and cannot establish the diagnosis.

VEMP testing evaluates saccular and utricular function (inferior and superior vestibular nerve respectively); it may be abnormal but is not a diagnostic imaging test and cannot definitively identify a schwannoma.
clinicalPearl: Any patient with unilateral unexplained sensorineural hearing loss and/or tinnitus must have an MRI IAM with gadolinium to exclude acoustic neuroma — the definitive gold-standard investigation.
keyClue: Unilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss, unilateral tinnitus, imbalance, Weber to the opposite (better) ear
commonExamTrap: Choosing ABR as the definitive test — ABR is a useful screening tool but MRI with gadolinium is the definitive and gold-standard investigation for acoustic neuroma.
missedConcept: Acoustic neuroma grows slowly from the Schwann cells of the vestibular nerve; unilateral SNHL + tinnitus + imbalance is the triad — MRI IAM is mandatory, as delay leads to larger tumour requiring more morbid surgery.
